nativeRange.habitat: Sand and rubble patches on steep seaward reef slopes; typically found at depths of 35–70 m (recorded range 30–85 m). Adults generally occur in pairs rather than aggregations.

decorPreferences: deep sand bed (at least 7–8 cm) for burrowing
mixed substrate of sand, coral pieces, shells, and rubble
live rock with rubble piles at the base

diet.notes: Generally fed a varied carnivore diet of mysis shrimp, finely chopped marine fish and crustacean flesh, krill, and zooplankton-type frozen foods. Has been reported to do best with multiple small feedings per day rather than a single large meal. Live foods (e.g., live mysis, baby brine) are commonly recommended early in acclimation to encourage newly-imported individuals to begin feeding.

avoidWith: conspecifics (except as an established male/female pair)
aggressive or boisterous fish (large angels, triggers, pugnacious damsels)
predators large enough to view it as prey
anemones (risk of stings to a sand-bound, shy fish)

imageCandidates.notes: Sharp, well-lit 5210x3473 photo of a live, vibrant magenta-purple Purple Tilefish; whole elongated body in frame, blue eye and red-tinged forked caudal fin clearly visible.

summary: A magenta-purple deepwater sand tilefish from the Western Pacific, generally considered an advanced-care species best kept as a monogamous pair in a quiet, peaceful reef aquarium with a deep sand bed for burrowing and a tightly covered lid.
careNotes:
The Purple Tilefish is a deepwater (~35–70 m) sand-dwelling species, and most husbandry challenges stem from that origin. Newly imported fish are commonly reported to be very sensitive during transport and acclimation; it is generally advised to acclimate them into a dimly lit, fully cycled, established aquarium with stable parameters and minimal competition from tankmates. Live foods are often used to encourage feeding in fish that have gone off food during shipping.

This species is widely reported as a strong jumper — a tight-fitting lid is generally considered mandatory, with no open gaps around overflows or hang-on-back equipment. It is also an obligate sand-dweller: a substrate of at least 7–8 cm consisting of mixed sand, coral rubble, and broken shell is generally advised so the fish can construct burrows. Bare-bottom tanks and shallow substrate setups are broadly considered unsuitable.

The Purple Tilefish is broadly described as peaceful toward other species but moderately aggressive toward its own kind. An established male/female pair tends to coexist well, but introducing a second unpaired individual to a tank already holding one is generally discouraged. In the wild, this species displays obligate monogamy — pairs form and hold together regardless of resource availability, which is relatively unusual among reef fishes — and pair spawning has been reported in well-maintained home aquariums.
breedingNotes: The Purple Tilefish has been reported to spawn successfully in well-maintained home aquariums, which is unusual for a marine fish collected from such depths. Because the species practices obligate monogamy in the wild, captive breeding generally requires an established male/female pair housed without competition from other conspecifics. Larval rearing has not been widely documented in the hobby literature and is generally considered difficult, and commercial captive-bred specimens are not commonly available.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source page was the dartfish category index; species-specific care content was sourced from the LiveAquaria Purple Tilefish product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/product/201/?pcatid=201). Provided tank size (50 gal), care level (moderate), temperament, reef-safe (yes), diet (carnivore), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), adult size (6 in), and the cautionary notes on jumping and substrate burrowing.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (5 in / 13 cm), care level ('Expert Only'), temperament (peaceful), reef-safe (yes), diet (carnivore), and Western Pacific / Solomon Islands native range. Did not provide water-parameter ranges.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Authoritative for taxonomy (family Malacanthidae, order Eupercaria), max size (13.0 cm TL), depth range (30–85 m, typically 35–70 m), wild temperature range (22–28°C), Indo-West Pacific distribution, habitat (sand and rubble patches on steep seaward slopes), and the noted obligate-monogamy pair-bonding behavior.

additional.name: ReefApp.net Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Specialist source. Recommends a larger minimum tank (400 L) than retailer sources (~190 L), characterises the species as delicate and shy, and confirms strong jumping behaviour, substrate burrowing preference, sensitivity to acclimation, susceptibility to marine ich when stressed, and best housing as a male/female pair.

additional.name: Marine Collectors
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (50 gal), care level (moderate/intermediate), temperament (peaceful), reef-safe (yes), diet (carnivore), and provided a slightly wider pH range (7.8–8.5) and salinity range (1.022–1.026). Adult-size figure of 8 in was treated as an outlier against FishBase's 13 cm TL.

fish.breedingNotes: Pair spawning has been reported in well-kept aquariums, though larval rearing remains poorly documented and commercial captive-bred stock is not generally available.
